The FI Formula: Explained

Published on January 2025 • Reading time: 5 minutes

Financial Independence (FI) isn’t about saving the most money—it’s about gaining the freedom to live life on your terms. The math is surprisingly simple. Let’s break down the FI formula and how you can calculate your number.

What Is the FI Formula?

Your FI number represents the amount of invested assets you need to live off indefinitely. It’s based on your annual expenses—not your income. Why? Because expenses dictate the lifestyle you want to maintain.

The 25x Rule

The general rule of thumb: multiply your annual expenses by 25. If your expenses are $40,000 a year, your FI number is $1,000,000. This assumes a 4% safe withdrawal rate (SWR), meaning you can withdraw 4% of your portfolio annually without running out of money over a 30-year horizon.

Why Expenses Matter More Than Income

Cutting expenses reduces the FI target, shortens your timeline, and lowers the amount you need to save. For example, reducing annual expenses from $50,000 to $40,000 decreases your FI number by $250,000.

How to Track Progress

Tracking net worth and savings rate is key. Tools like our Net Worth Tracker and Early Retirement Estimator help you stay on course and adjust as needed.

Example

If your annual expenses are $50,000, your FI target is $1.25 million. With $200,000 already saved and investing $30,000/year at 7% growth, you could hit FI in roughly 15 years.
